ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'coffee beverage' class; no specific entry for Kopi O (Nanyang-style black coffee with sugar). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers black coffee generically; no specific Kopi O entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34731007 refers to coffee as a substance; no specific Kopi O concept exists. Closest parent used.

main_ingredients: Robusta coffee beans (dark roasted, often with sugar and butter/margarine during roasting)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of Kopi O as served in a Malaysian or Singaporean kopitiam, approximately 200 ml, typically containing brewed black Robusta coffee sweetened with white sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Kopi O (Coffee, black, with sugar) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / HPB Singapore nutrient reference for sweetened black coffee
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kopi O is a standard Nanyang-style black coffee brewed from dark-roasted Robusta beans and sweetened with approximately 10–12 g of white sugar per 200 ml cup. Nutrient values per 100 g are derived from HPB Singapore Foodo data for sweetened black coffee and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for kopi hitam. Energy reflects sugar contribution (~4 kcal/g) plus negligible coffee solids. Per 100 ml (≈100 g): energy ~20 kcal, carbohydrate ~5 g (from sugar), protein ~0.1 g, fat ~0 g. Values scaled to per-100 g basis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of black coffee and sugar-sweetened coffee beverages — University of Sydney GI Database reference; black coffee GI effectively zero; sugar contribution modelled separately
gi_source.source_match_notes: Black coffee has a negligible glycemic response and is conventionally assigned GI near zero. The University of Sydney GI Database lists black coffee as having no meaningful glycemic effect. Kopi O contains approximately 5 g sugar per 100 ml; the sugar (sucrose) has a GI of approximately 65, but at this dilution the overall beverage GI is very low. Per convention for essentially zero-GI beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (lowest valid positive value). glycemic_load_per_serving = (5 g carbohydrate × 1 / 100) × 200 g serving ≈ 0.1, rounded to 0.0 for practical purposes. gi_category is low.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~2 mg per 100 g); caffeine may cause transient blood pressure elevation
hypertension.guidance: Kopi O is very low in sodium and poses minimal hypertension risk from a dietary sodium perspective; however, individuals with caffeine sensitivity or uncontrolled hypertension should limit intake to 1–2 cups per day as caffeine can transiently raise blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (~5 g per 100 ml) contributes to glycemic load; unsweetened version (Kopi O Kosong) preferred for glycemic management
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request Kopi O Kosong (without sugar) to eliminate the added sugar contribution, as the standard Kopi O contains approximately 10 g of sugar per 200 ml serving which can raise postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; moderate caffeine intake associated with neutral to mildly beneficial cardiovascular outcomes in epidemiological studies
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kopi O contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and is generally considered low risk for cardiovascular disease; moderate consumption of 1–3 cups per day is consistent with cardiovascular health guidelines, though individuals on antihypertensive or anticoagulant medications should consult their physician regarding caffeine intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kopi O (also written Coffee 'O' or Kopi-O) is a traditional Nanyang-style black coffee popular in Malaysia and Singapore, served without milk and sweetened with white sugar. It is brewed using dark-roasted Robusta coffee beans — typically roasted with sugar and butter or margarine to develop a distinctive caramelised, bold flavour — and filtered through a cloth sock strainer. The resulting beverage is strong, slightly bitter, and aromatic, with a characteristic thick body compared to Western-style drip coffee. Kopi O is a staple of the kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) culture across Malaysia and Singapore and is one of the most commonly ordered beverages at hawker centres and local cafés. Nutritionally, Kopi O is a low-calorie beverage providing approximately 20 kcal per 100 ml, with the caloric content derived almost entirely from the added sugar (~5 g carbohydrate per 100 ml). Fat and protein content are negligible. It contains small amounts of potassium, niacin, and phosphorus from the coffee itself. The beverage is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and halal diets. For individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, the unsweetened variant — Kopi O Kosong — is strongly preferred to avoid the glycemic impact of added sugar. Caffeine content is moderate to high due to the use of Robusta beans, which have higher caffeine levels than Arabica. The glycemic index of the beverage is effectively zero when consumed without sugar, but the standard sweetened version carries a modest glycemic load from the dissolved sucrose.
common_user_queries: Is Kopi O the same as black coffee?
How many calories in a cup of Kopi O?
Is Kopi O suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between Kopi O and Kopi O Kosong?
What type of coffee beans are used in Kopi O?
meal_context: paired with kaya toast or roti bakar

confidence_reason: Kopi O is a well-documented Nanyang-style beverage in Malaysian and Singaporean food culture. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD references for sweetened black coffee, as no single exact database entry for 'Kopi O' with standardised sugar content exists across all approved sources. The adaptation is defensible and consistent with the known composition of the beverage.
notes: 1. fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero — black coffee brewed without milk or cream contains no meaningful fat; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and IMR MyFCD references for black coffee. 2. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g are 0.0: naturally zero for a fat-free beverage. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero — brewed coffee liquid contains no dietary fibre. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-derived beverage. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per source; coffee contains negligible vitamin C. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per source. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-derived beverage. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — not present in coffee. 9. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1: not reported in the primary source database for this beverage. 10. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1: not reported in the primary source database for this beverage. 11. vitamin_b6_mg is -1: not reported in the primary source database for this beverage. 12. folate_mcg_dfe is -1: not reported in the primary source database for this beverage. 13. vitamin_e_mg is -1: not reported in the primary source database for this beverage. 14. zinc_mg is -1: not reported in the primary source database for this beverage. 15. selenium_mcg is -1: not reported in the primary source database for this beverage. 16. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates from the coffee itself; the added sugar is dilute; gi_category low per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 reflecting the negligible practical glycemic load of a standard serving. 17. Cuisine is listed as Malaysian in the input; Kopi O is equally a Singaporean kopitiam staple. Multilingual naming follows Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is a shared Malaysian-Singaporean cultural item and Singapore rules are more stringent. 18. The standard serving assumes sugar is added (traditional preparation); Kopi O Kosong (without sugar) would have energy_kcal ~2 and carbohydrate_g ~0.5 per 100 g.
